data.title: 2030 census poses tough questions for Democrats' future
data.description:
The population shifts projected for the next decade are raising questions about the impact they could have on Democrats’ pathway to winning the White House and a House majority starting in 2032. 

2030 population estimates released by the U.S. Census Bureau last month suggest a handful of Sun Belt states are expected to gain seats in the House, while some prominent Democratic-led states, like New York and California, are expected to lose seats. 

While experts emphasize the numbers are only estimates and don’t account for potential political shifts, the population estimates suggest the party may have to grapple with how to become more competitive in Republican-held states like Texas and Florida. 

“They have to expand the electoral battlefield from the traditional states that they have been most comfortable in campaigning in, and that means reengaging in states like Texas and in Florida, for starters, because you’re going to see a greater number of electoral weight assigned to even those two states,” said Miami-based Democratic pollster Fernand Amandi, who worked on former President Obama’s presidential campaigns in Florida. 

“They need to reengage in that posture again, otherwise they’re going to have a very difficult time recapturing the White House,” he added.

U.S. Census Bureau population estimates released last month have led experts to suggest Florida and Texas could gain a small handful of seats in the next decade. Several other Sun Belt states, like Georgia and Arizona, could also notch additional seats.  

California and New York are expected to lose a handful of seats; other states like Pennsylvania, Illinois, Wisconsin and Oregon are likely to lose a seat or two. 

Experts caution that there’s only so much observers can read into the population estimates.  

For one, the numbers in different states obviously can change between now and 2030. Estimates also don’t account for an ever-changing political landscape — the same states and voting blocs that trended red, blue or purple may not remain that way by 2030. 

“Things are sufficiently politically fluid for partisans right now that I sure wouldn’t want to be making any bets as to which states are going to be red states and which states are going to be blue states six years [from now],” said Justin Levitt, a professor at Loyola Marymount University Loyola Law School who closely tracks redistricting. 

Most importantly, a lot of this is predicated on how well the 2030 Census is carried out. If states’ populations aren’t counted correctly, the ramifications for states are significant, including how many House seats states are apportioned for the upcoming decade. Red and blue states alike have an incentive for accurate counts. 

Still, the estimates offer some early clues — and warning signs — ahead of 2032. For Democrats, the expected population increases in Florida and Texas mean those states will only become more important for the party’s Electoral College math. 

“Looking at the 2024 presidential election, Trump defeated Harris 312-226 in the Electoral College, reaching (and surpassing) the 270 needed for a majority and victory,” wrote Geoffrey Skelley, chief elections analyst for Decision Desk HQ, in a Substack post.  

“The projected seat changes would have increased Trump’s electoral vote haul to 322, a 10-vote increase.” 

Monique Alcala, a former executive director for the Texas Democratic Party, suggested that if the party failed to act there, “national Democrats are going to have no other choice but to … piece together that puzzle outside of Texas.”  

Alcala said that in order for Democrats to become more competitive in the Lone Star State, Democrats need a strong state party, a strong donor table and strong campaign committees for the legislative chambers, with the task of recruiting high-quality candidates and “building an apparatus that trains them in order to run professionalized races.” 

The population estimates also offer a bit of a murky picture regarding the House majority. Paul Mitchell, a California Democratic redistricting expert, suggested one of the consequences could be the party losing a sizable number of seats in the Golden State for Democrats. 

“The House majority would essentially take two hits out of California, one being the reapportionment and loss of congressional districts to other states, and the second being the reversion back to a commission-drawn map, which I 100 percent believe is going to be the lines that members of Congress are going to be running under in 2032,” Mitchell said. 

A map drawn under the state’s independent redistricting commission would likely make the maps more competitive between the parties. 

While there are some signs that certain Southern states like Florida are trending redder — the Florida GOP has made gains in its voter registration — it’s not entirely clear how the population shifts will impact the political landscape in Republican-held states.  

Some Democrats believe there’s reason to be optimistic about the changing landscape in other states. 

“When you look at places like Texas, which no one would dispute is growing, the most interesting information is that the communities of color are growing and the urban centers are growing,” said John Bisognano, president of the National Democratic Redistricting Committee and its affiliates. 

“It’s not just generically that a red state, as one would call Texas, is growing. It’s that, from my perspective, that state is purpling and changing,” he added. 

The 2030 estimates come at a critical time for Democrats, who have overperformed in elections over the past year while also navigating without a clear political leader. 

The progressive-centrist battle within the party has only increased in recent months as the Democrats grapple with the direction of the party, particularly as calls for generational change have reverberated loudly and as both parties look toward a post-Trump political era. 

While the 2030 population estimates offered both warning signs and some reasons to be optimistic for Democrats, the full picture of the political landscape is still taking shape. 

“I think what we may be looking at on the horizon is maybe a red wall where Democrats are going to have to break into the … South or …. break into the Sun Belt states in order to win the White House, whereas Republicans may not need to win a Rust Belt state in order to carry the day,” said Adam Kincaid, president of the National Republican Redistricting Trust. 

“I don’t think it means that either party is going to have a lock on the White House. I just think that it’s going to change,” he added. “It’ll just change our campaigns like apportionment always does.”

data.title: GOP senators express relief over failed DOJ effort to indict Democrats
data.description:
Key Republican senators say a grand jury made the right decision by rejecting an effort by the Department of Justice (DOJ) to indict six Democratic lawmakers, including Sens. Mark Kelly (Ariz.) and Elissa Slotkin (Mich.), on criminal charges for filming a video last year urging military service members to disobey unlawful orders.

Republican senators by and large agree the video urging members of the military and the nation’s intelligence agencies to refuse unlawful orders was inappropriate because they thought it encouraged insubordination.

But GOP lawmakers were surprised by the bombshell revelation Tuesday that Jeanine Pirro, the U.S. attorney for the District of Columbia, had attempted to secure a criminal indictment against Kelly, Slotkin and other Democrats, and they expressed relief that the aggressive move failed.

“I think the grand jury made the right decision,” said Senate Armed Services Committee Chair Roger Wicker (R-Miss.), whose panel includes Kelly and Slotkin as members.

Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.), a member of the Judiciary Committee, offered stronger words, applauding the grand jury’s decision to reject what he called “lawfare.”

He suggested the attempts to indict his Democratic colleagues were driven by politics after President Trump last year called for Kelly and Slotkin to be “arrested and put on trial” because of the video.  

“Political lawfare waged by either side undermines America’s criminal justice system, which is the gold standard of the world. Thankfully in this instance a jury saw the attempted indictments for what they really were,” Tillis said in a statement posted to social media.

“Political lawfare is not normal, not acceptable, and needs to stop,” he warned.

Senate Judiciary Committee Chair Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa) said federal prosecutors have better things to do than bringing charges against sitting U.S. senators over their political speech.

“I think that our law enforcement people ought to be spending their time on making our communities safe and going after real lawbreakers,” he said.

Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska) said bringing criminal charges against sitting senators over the video was “reckless” and “punitive.”

“I’m glad that the grand jury came back and rejected indictments but I think it is a very disturbing direction that the administration has taken in basically trying to make criminals out of sitting lawmakers who effectively pointed out what the uniform military code of justice says,” she said.

Asked about the grand jury’s rejection of the charges, Sen. Mike Rounds (R-S.D.) said the “system worked the way it’s supposed to.” 

A Republican senator who requested anonymity said the attempt to bring criminal charges against Democratic lawmakers appeared to violate the Constitution’s Speech or Debate Clause.

Article I, Section 6, Clause 1 of the Constitution states federal lawmakers shall be privileged from arrest during their attendance of Congress or while traveling to or from the Capitol.

“There’s the Constitution, it’s called the Speech and Debate clause. It trumps statute,” the GOP lawmaker stated.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) said he trusts the grand jury’s judgment and predicted its rejection of the indictment should put an end to attempts to criminally prosecute Democratic lawmakers.

“It looks like the grand jury came to their conclusion. I trust our judicial system. If that’s the conclusion they arrived at, I think that pretty much lays things to rest as far as I’m concerned,” he told reporters.

Slotkin and Kelly told reporters Wednesday they only learned of the attempt by Pirro’s office to indict them from the media and were never told what charges they potentially faced or what statute they allegedly violated.

“We literally are finding out from the media and we have not been formally told what they were trying to charge us with and what law they were using,” Slotkin said.

She thanked Tillis for speaking out against the prosecution attempt.

Kelly said he and his colleagues may never know what specific criminal charges the Justice Department tried to bring against them.

“[With a] grand jury, you do not get to present a legal defense. It’s only the prosecutor and the jurors and it’s secret,” he said. “We don’t ever expect to find out.”

The Arizona Democrat pointed out that “it is very rare” that a grand jury refuses to indict the target of a criminal prosecution, noting that in 2013, the most recent year for which he was able to obtain data, there were 165,000 indictments and only five were not successful.

“This process has a 99.997 [percent] success rate — yes, I did the math,” he quipped.

Sen. Richard Blumenthal (D-Conn.), a member of the Senate Judiciary Committee, called the attempt to bring criminal charges against Kelly, Slotkin and other Democrats elected to Congress “absolutely unprecedented and outrageous.”

He argued Democrats were “exercising the right of free speech.”

“It is very simply an effort to silence and intimidate public officials and everyday Americans who are critical of this administration. I think it is a perversion of justice,” he said.

Blumenthal, a former U.S. attorney and state attorney general, said the grand jury’s rejection of the indictment was an embarrassment for the Trump Justice Department.

“For a grand jury to refuse the request for an indictment tells me that they had no evidence, no law, nothing on their side,” he said.

Slotkin told reporters Tuesday she thinks Pirro brought the prosecution against Democratic lawmakers because of pressure from Trump.

“This is not about real law, it’s about weaponizing the Justice Department against people you disagree with,” she said.

The president vented his fury over the video that the Democrats circulated in November urging military service members to refuse unlawful military orders, which came at a time when lawmakers in both parties were raising questions about the legality of the Trump administration’s missile strikes against speed boats suspected of smuggling drugs from Venezuela.

Trump accused the Democrats in a social media post of “seditious behavior, punishable by death.” He also called them “traitors,” despite the fact that Kelly flew 39 combat missions over Iraq during Operation Desert Storm and Slotkin worked for approximately four years as a CIA analyst focusing on the Middle East.

The president’s vehement reaction and over-the-top attacks left some Republican senators dumbfounded.

“If you take it at face value, the idea that calling your opponents traitors — and then specifically saying that it warrants the death penalty — is reckless, inappropriate, irresponsible,” Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) told CBS’s “Face the Nation” last year.

Republican senators have also cautioned Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth against trying to punish Kelly, a retired Navy captain, by taking administrative steps to reduce the senator’s military rank and pension.

Senate Appropriations Committee Chair Susan Collins (R-Maine), who has jurisdiction over the Pentagon’s budget, last month said it would not be “appropriate” to target Kelly’s retired rank or pension because of his participation in the video.

Al Weaver contributed.

data.title: Biden-pardoned Maduro ally again targeted by DOJ
data.description:
A close ally of Venezuela’s former regime leader is being targeted by the Department of Justice (DOJ) after receiving a pardon during the Biden administration.

Alex Saab, Venezuela’s former Minister of Industry and National Production, is being investigated by DOJ officials for an alleged bribery conspiracy to import food,  two former law enforcement officials told the Associated Press on the condition of anonymity due to the ongoing legal probe.

Prosecutors are reviewing Saab’s potential involvement in regime leader Nicolas Maduro’s scheme to import rice, corn flour and cooking oil for poor Venezuelans at high rates in exchange for a kickback. Sales date back to 2016 when Saab’s longtime partner, Alvaro Pulido, launched an effort to import 10 million food boxes from Mexico at $34 per box, despite knowing the real cost was far less, according to AP.

DOJ officials investigated the so-called CLAP program backed by Maduro and indicted Saab in 2019 on charges related to a separate bribery scheme to build low-income houses. He was arrested in 2020 but ultimately received a pardon from former President Biden through a prisoner swap deal, in exchange for the release of several Americans jailed in Venezuela.

The renewed interest in Saab’s potential involvement in the food conspiracy follows the Trump administration’s capture of Maduro.

Delcy Rodriguez has been serving as the acting president of Venezuela amid Maduro’s capture. Rodriguez has never been formally accused of wrongdoing by the U.S., and upon taking power, she removed Saab from the presidential cabinet.

Saab is suspected to be residing inside Venezuela with his exact whereabouts unknown per AP. Luigi Giuliano, an Italian attorney, said he met Saab last week in the Venezuelan capital of Caracas and denied he was detained but would not share further comment, according to the newswire.

Some say that if Saab is found, he could provide crucial testimony against Maduro due to his years-long business and political relationship with the regime leader.

“The indictment against Maduro contained a lot of salacious allegations, but there was little in the way of corroboration,” David Weinstein, a former federal prosecutor in Miami, told AP. “Saab, if the reports about his own criminal activity and closeness to Maduro are true, can describe for jurors a range of criminal activity that is alleged to have taken place across Maduro’s government.”

data.title: IRS improperly disclosed immigrant tax data to DHS: Report
data.description:
The Internal Revenue Service (IRS) improperly disclosed confidential tax data of thousands of people to the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) as part of the Trump administration’s push to crack down on immigration enforcement measures across agencies. 

Last April, DHS signed a memorandum of understanding with the Treasury Department so that Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officials could request the names and addresses of individuals suspected to be living in the U.S. without legal status.

Immigrants have for years paid taxes even if they are not in the U.S. legally, with the federal government assuring them that their data would be protected.

A Wednesday declaration from IRS Chief Risk and Control Officer Dottie Romo said the tax agency has verified roughly 47,000 of the 1.28 million names DHS requested, according to the Associated Press.

Less than 5 percent of individuals had additional personal information shared with immigration authorities, which potentially violates federal law protecting taxpayer data, per the newswire. The Washington Post first reported the erroneous disclosure, citing three people familiar with the situation. They told the newspaper that the error was only recently discovered and is working with other federal agencies on a response.

The IRS and ICE did not immediately respond to The Hill’s request for comment on the matter.

A DHS spokesperson told The Hill that DHS is “finally doing what it should have all along” by sharing information across the federal government to “solve problems.”

“Information sharing across agencies is essential to identify who is in our country, including violent criminals, determine what public safety and terror threats may exist so we can neutralize them, scrub these individuals from voter rolls, and identify what public benefits these aliens are using at taxpayer expense,” the spokesperson added.

However, immigration advocates say the move is an affront to citizens and immigrants without legal status alike.

Tom Bowman, policy counsel for the Center for Democracy and Technology said “the improper sharing of taxpayer data is unsafe, unlawful, and subject to serious criminal penalties.”

“Once taxpayer data is opened to immigration enforcement, mistakes are inevitable and the consequences fall on innocent people,” Tom Bowman, policy counsel for the Center for Democracy & Technology said in a statement to AP. 

”The disclosure of thousands of confidential records unfortunately shows precisely why strict legal firewalls exist and have — until now — been treated as an important guardrail,” he added.

Other groups immediately raised concerns last year after the MOU between DHS and the Treasury was signed.

“This unprecedented agreement represents a drastic shift away from the IRS’s longstanding commitment to taxpayer privacy. Taxpayer information is strictly confidential, with IRS privacy laws only permitting information-sharing with law enforcement authorities in very specific and narrow circumstances to help in certain criminal investigations,” the National Immigration Forum wrote.

“Acting IRS Commissioner Melanie Krause, who was appointed by President Trump earlier in 2025, resigned in protest over the signed agreement. Other top IRS officials, including chief privacy officer Kathleen Walters and chief privacy officer Teresa Hunter, also resigned over concerns the MOU likely violates taxpayer privacy law,” the advocacy organization added.

data.title: 'Dawson's Creek' star James Van Der Beek dies at 48
data.description:
James Van Der Beek, the hit star from “Dawson’s Creek,” died to colorectal cancer on Wednesday. He was 48 years old.

“Our beloved James David Van Der Beek passed peacefully this morning. He met his final days with courage, faith, and grace,” his family wrote in a statement on Instagram.

“There is much to share regarding his wishes, love for humanity, and the sacredness of time. Those days will come. For now we ask for peaceful privacy as we grieve our loving husband, father, son, brother, and friend,” they added.

Van Der Beek was first diagnosed in August 2023, according to TODAY.

“I’ve been privately dealing with this diagnosis and have been taking steps to resolve it, with the support of my incredible family,” the critically acclaimed actor told People in 2024.

“There’s reason for optimism, and I’m feeling good,” he added.

Van Der Beek was well known for his role in the hit “Dawson’s Creek” series, where teens explored the realities of friendship and love.

He landed several award-winning roles after the show including the 1999 film “Varsity Blues,” for which he won an MTV Movie Award for Best Breakthrough Male Performance, and a 2001 appearance in “Scary Movie,” which earned him an MTV Movie Award for Best Cameo in a Movie.

Later down the line, Van Der Beek joined the 28th season of “Dancing with the Stars” with partner Emma Slater. The two became semifinalists and fan favorites.

Van Der Beek’s acting career was nurtured during his teenage years at Cheshire Academy, where he shined in onstage performances. His talent got him a scholarship to Drew University, where he sang in an all-male a cappella group before leaving school to join the cast on “Dawson’s Creek.”

“The more you make fun of yourself and don’t try to go for any kind of respect, the more people seem to respect you,” he told Vanity Fair in 2011.

“I’ve always been a clown trapped in a leading man’s body.”

Van Der Beek is survived by his six children and wife, according to The Associated Press.

data.title: Trump threatens 'consequences' for Republicans who voted against tariffs on Canada
data.description:
President Trump threatened “consequences come Election time” for Republican lawmakers who oppose his administration’s tariffs on U.S. trading partners.

“Any Republican, in the House or the Senate, that votes against TARIFFS will seriously suffer the consequences come Election time, and that includes Primaries!” the president wrote in a Truth Social post Wednesday evening, shortly after six House Republicans voted to repeal the president’s tariffs on Canada. 

Reps. Don Bacon (R-Neb.), Kevin Kiley (R-Calif.), Thomas Massie (R-Ky.), Jeff Hurd (R-Colo.), Brian Fitzpatrick (R-Pa.) and Dan Newhouse (R-Wash.) joined Democrats in supporting the resolution sponsored by Rep. Gregory Meeks (D-N.Y.), the ranking member of the House Foreign Affairs Committee. Rep. Jared Goldman (Maine) was the sole Democrat to vote against the measure.

The president has made tariffs a central economic policy during his second term, hiking up import taxes on goods from some of the U.S.’s closest trading partners, including Canada, Mexico and China. 

These decisions have strained international relations with these countries and faced broad opposition from Democratic lawmakers. The nonpartisan Tax Foundation estimated that these tariffs resulted in an average tax increase of $1,000 per U.S. household last year — a cost that could increase by several hundred dollars this year if these policies stay intact, the group said.

Trump doubled down on his use of tariffs to negotiate new deals with U.S. trading partners in his Wednesday post. 

“In addition, TARIFFS have given us Great National Security because the mere mention of the word has Countries agreeing to our strongest wishes,” he wrote. “TARIFFS have given us Economic and National Security, and no Republican should be responsible for destroying this privilege.”

Democratic Governors Association communications director Sam Newton called out several House Republican gubernatorial candidates who voted in favor of Trump’s tariffs in a Wednesday statement.

“As Americans continue to bear the brunt of high costs on everything from housing to groceries, every congressional Republican running for governor voted to defend Trump’s deeply unpopular, cost-raising tariffs on Canada,” Newton wrote. “Republican candidates got blown out of the water in New Jersey and Virginia in 2025 because Americans are tired of their disastrous economic agenda.

“Today’s vote only worsens the pain American families and businesses are already feeling and helps ensure the headwinds facing Republicans are going to be even worse in governors’ races across the country in 2026,” Newton continued.

data.title: GOP voting bill clears House as conservatives clamor for Senate action
data.description:
House Republicans clamoring for stricter voting rules cleared a hurdle with passage of the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility (SAVE) America Act on Wednesday — but that’s only ramping up debate among conservatives on how to pressure the Senate to take up the election legislation.

The bill, which would require individuals to present proof of citizenship to register to vote and voters to present identification when casting a ballot, passed the House in a 218-213 vote on Wednesday.

Now, conservatives are debating tactics for how to get it through the Senate — and overcome the chamber’s rules that typically require support from Democrats to move legislation past the 60-vote cloture threshold.

Ideas include organizing an intense public pressure campaign; pushing the Senate Republicans to band together and revive the “talking filibuster” in hopes of wearing down Democratic opposition; or attaching the bill to a must-pass piece of legislation and daring them to reject it.

Rep. Keith Self (R-Texas) is hoping to see President Trump — who has the largest bully pulpit of all — ramp up calls for senators to act.

“The president has to bring every possible weapon he has to this fight to get the Senate to move, because the Senate will not move without incredible, crushing pressure,” Self said.

Conservative organizer Scott Presler on Wednesday spoke to the Republican Study Committee, the largest conservative caucus in the House, encouraging them to keep up the heat on Senate Republicans — and arguing that passing more conservative priorities is essential for keeping the House.

“If the leadership is only going to run on milquetoast tax cuts, you are going to lose this November,” Presler told the members.

“I’m not here to cause controversy, to cause any sort of drama. But let’s be clear: You guys have done your job. The Senate is not doing its job. And who has the most to lose from them not passing legislation? Y’all.” 

Republican Study Committee Chair August Pfluger (R-Texas) concurred.

“The American people did not give Republicans a mandate to make excuses. They gave us one to deliver wins, and the SAVE America Act is exactly that,” he said. “The Senate must get this to President Trump’s desk without further delay. The American people are watching and they are done waiting.”

One strategy that is unlikely to work is trying to convince Democrats to vote yes. 

Republicans advocating for the bill said Democrats should support it because there is broad support among Americans for voter ID — which had 83 percent support in a 2025 Pew Research survey — and accused those who do not support a proof of citizenship requirement of wanting noncitizens to illegally vote. Investigations and studies have found only rare confirmed instances of noncitizen voting.

But the measure has only lost support from Democrats as Republicans have pushed harder for it. While four moderate Democrats — Reps. Ed Case (Hawaii), Henry Cuellar (Texas), Jared Golden (Maine), and Marie Gluesenkamp Perez (Wash.) — had supported a previous version of the legislation last year, only Cuellar voted in favor Wednesday.

The previous version of the legislation did not include a voter ID requirement — which says mail-in ballots should be submitted with a photocopy of a state-issued ID, or an affidavit swearing they are unable to obtain a copy of valid ID along with the last four digits of their Social Security number. Gluesenkamp Perez highlighted a provision of the bill directing governments to provide access to free photocopying, saying to Republicans: “You have not written a good piece of legislation.”

That isn’t keeping conservatives in the House and beyond from brainstorming ways to push it forward in the Senate.

Rep. Anna Paulina Luna (R-Fla.) brought the SAVE America Act to the center of a government funding fight prompted by Democrats demanding reforms to immigration enforcement, initially pitching a plan to send the funding package back with the voting legislation attached and daring the Senate to extend a partial government shutdown by rejecting it.

She backed down from that push after a meeting with President Trump, before saying she was optimistic about the Senate utilizing a “standing filibuster” or “talking filibuster,” which would force Democrats to continuously talk on the floor as they attempt to block the voting bill. Sen. Mike Lee (R-Utah), the lead sponsor of the bill in the Senate, has also advocated for such a move.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) has said he wants to put the SAVE America Act on the floor but has made clear that there is not “anywhere near close” to enough support for using the “nuclear option” on the Senate’s 60-vote threshold to advance the legislation with only Republican votes.

And he has signaled reluctance to the idea of using the “talking filibuster,” because it has an “opportunity cost” in that it “ties up the floor indefinitely.”

“Is there another way of getting there? We’ll see,” Thune said this week.

The measure also doesn’t have unanimous support among Senate Republicans.

Sen. Lisa Murkowski (Alaska) on Wednesday became the first Senate Republican to speak out against passing the bill.

“When Democrats attempted to advance sweeping election reform legislation in 2021, Republicans were unanimous in opposition because it would have federalized elections, something we have long opposed. Now, I’m seeing proposals such as the SAVE Act and MEGA that would effectively do just that,” she wrote in a statement posted to social media.

“Once again, I do not support these efforts,” she added.

Members in response have started eyeing other avenues to try to force the Senate to act on the bill.

“If they won’t just take it up straight away, then I think we’ll have to figure out ways to put pressure on them to bring it as a part of must-pass legislation,” said Rep. John Rose (R-Tenn.), who held up a vote on a funding bill over the issue of the SAVE Act last week.

Luna has floated tacking the bill onto an upcoming reauthorization of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act.

“Your continued refusal to return the Senate and the use of the filibuster to its original intent so we can pass the SAVE (AMERICA) Act will have consequences and force us in the House to use every leverage we have to get it passed one way or another,” Luna posted on the social platform X.

Presler, the conservative organizer, said it is a bigger priority for the Senate to have a floor debate on the matter than to only seek an avenue to get the bill to Trump’s desk.

“I want it to pass,” Presler said. “But the American people would rather [have] action on it than allowing the House to do its part, and to have inaction from the Senate and just have it stall.”

“We demand action,” Presler said.

Asked by Fox Business host Maria Bartiromo on Wednesday if the bill would make it through the Senate, Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) said: “I certainly hope so.

“We’re going to do everything we can to make that happen and get it to the president’s desk. As you know, it’s a top priority of his and ours,” Johnson said. 

“I’m hopeful that Leader Thune and our Senate Republicans can get it done,” Johnson said.

data.title: Buddhist Monks close out 'Walk for Peace' on National Mall
data.description:
A group of Buddhist monks marched down the length of the National Mall in Washington, D.C., on Wednesday afternoon, concluding their “Walk for Peace” that began 109 days ago in Fort Worth, Texas.

Venerable Bhikkhu Pannakara, who has led the group over the course of their 2,300 mile pilgrimage, addressed thousands of people gathered in front of the Lincoln Memorial.

“Ladies and gentlemen, life moves very fast,” Pannakara said. “Sometimes, just a few seconds are enough for us to hurt someone, to say words we regret or to create more pain in a world that is already exhausted.”

“But those same few seconds, if we live them well, can become the beginning of peace,” he continued. “Today, I do not ask you to think about big ideas. I simply invite you to live five seconds of your life with mindfulness.”

Two dozen monks began the journey on Oct. 26, traversing 8 states on their way to D.C., encountering harsh winter conditions along the way. The group has gained a nationwide following, sharing live updates on their social media and attracting crowds along their route.

Supporters at the mall on Wednesday held up flowers and signs in support of the travelers with messages like “Peaceful resistance” and “Mindfulness powers peace.”

The group’s government liaison and press coordinator, Dr. Neeraj Bajracharya, said that D.C. is “not the final destination” and urged the crowd at the Lincoln Memorial on Wednesday to take the walk’s message of peace with them.

“The walk for peace is going to continue,” Bajracharya said. “Washington, D.C., is not the final stop because the walk towards peace must continue.”

Several supporters at the Wednesday event in D.C. traveled to the capital city to witness the walk in person.

Tom and Donna Haddon traveled from Nags Head, North Carolina. They held up a floral cardboard peace sign as they waited for the closing ceremony on the mall on Wednesday.

Tom Haddon called the Lincoln Memorial a “fitting place” to end the walk. 

“The world is in a point of crisis, and this is such a positive message and I think it’s galvanized a lot of people,” he said. “Hopefully, the politicians somehow hear this, in some way, and we’re here because we don’t want any more war.”

Donna Haddon said they previously saw the group passing through Richmond, Virginia and Dunwoody, Georgia, and that she’s met other supporters who traveled to D.C. from California, Chicago and New Jersey to see the monks finish out their walk.

On Thursday, the group will reach their ninth state, Maryland, where they will board a bus back to Texas. Upon their arrival in Fort Worth on Saturday, the monks will complete their final 6 miles to the walk’s origin point.

Martha Brettschneider, a supporter from Vienna, Va. who joined the crowd at the Lincoln Memorial on Wednesday, called the monks’ journey “very inspiring and very hopeful.”

“It’s really a message of hope, and we’ve got this,” Brettschneider said. “And they are the ultimate reminder of that.”

Hundreds of supporters lined up along the mall on Wednesday to watch the walkers as they passed by. 

Timothy Bullock, a Buddhist monk based in D.C., walked alongside the group on the sidewalk. Bullock said he’s participated in several peace walks himself. 

“It does take a lot to get up every day, especially in this very cold weather and to set out, but I think that the energy of the people along the way really helps to fuel them and give them the strength to move on, to make that next step,” he said. 

Bullock noted the significance of the walk ending in D.C. during a turbulent time in American politics. 

“If there’s anywhere in the U.S. that could benefit from a huge spark of spiritual energy, and especially peaceful energy, it’s Washington, D.C.”

Pannakara ended his Wednesday speech by thanking everyone who supported the monks along their journey, including the local officials and law enforcement agencies in each state they traveled through. 

“Thank you for all the love, all of the hospitality, all of your support, even in bad conditions, in bad weather, freezing weather, cold rain,” Pannakara said.

“Thank you so much, and this moment will live with me for the rest of my life.”

data.title: 6 Republicans join Democrats to rebuke tariffs on Canada in blow to Trump
data.description:
Six House Republicans joined Democrats on Wednesday to pass a resolution to repeal President Trump’s tariffs on Canada, a blow to both President Trump and House GOP leadership.

The Wednesday 219-211 vote also followed a minor intraparty revolt against leadership over the administration’s handling of trade policy a day earlier.

Reps. Don Bacon (R-Neb.), Kevin Kiley (R-Calif.), Thomas Massie (R-Ky.), Jeff Hurd (R-Colo.), Brian Fitzpatrick (R-Pa.) and Dan Newhouse (R-Wash.) joined almost all Democrats in supporting the resolution sponsored by Rep. Gregory Meeks (D-N.Y.), the ranking member of the House Foreign Affairs Committee.

The resolution would terminate Trump’s use of a national emergency to impose punitive measures on Canada. 

Rep. Jared Golden (Maine) was the only Democrat to vote “no.”

“We have a trade agreement, and I think they’ve been a good ally, and I think they’ve been unfairly attacked by the administration, and so I’m going to oppose it — I’m going to oppose the tariffs,” Bacon told The Hill before the vote.

Massie has argued on the social platform X that “taxing authority is vested in the House of Representatives, not the Executive.”

Bacon, Massie and Kiley all voted with Democrats to block a procedural rule Tuesday evening in protest of included language that would have barred a member’s ability to call snap votes to repeal Trump’s tariffs.

The failed rule vote on Tuesday paved the way for a slew of snap repeal votes to be called up — starting with Wednesday’s.

Meeks’s resolution now heads to the Senate, where it appears likely to pass. In October, four Republicans joined all Democrats in a vote to terminate Trump’s tariffs on Canada, a measure led by Sen. Tim Kaine (D-Va.). Those GOP lawmakers were Sens. Mitch McConnell (Ky.), Rand Paul (Ky.), Susan Collins (Maine) and Lisa Murkowski (Alaska). 

While it’s presumed Trump will veto Meeks’s measure, Democrats are likely to capitalize on the president having to confront major pushback on his strategy of using tariffs to impose American demands on other countries.

Meeks had previously said in a statement that Trump’s tariffs are “driving up prices, killing jobs, and threatening our economy. This isn’t what Americans voted for.”

Meeks also has resolutions to terminate the national emergencies justifying Trump’s tariffs on other countries, such as Mexico, and the broad “Liberation Day” tariffs across the globe.

Laura Kelly contributed to this report.

data.title: World of Travel: Cappadocia and Bodrum — Inside Turkey's Hidden Cities and Lost Wonders
data.description:
Turkey has long been defined by its ability to connect worlds.

In Istanbul, that connection plays out between continents. But deeper inside the country and along its southwestern coast, that bridge reveals itself differently. Underground cities carved for survival. Ancient wonders built to immortalize love. Coastal towns where history still floats on the water.

This episode of World of Travel takes us from the volcanic valleys of Cappadocia to the turquoise coves of Bodrum, two regions that prove transformation is not always about what is new. Sometimes it is about what endures.

Cappadocia: The Landscape That Learned to Protect Itself

Before Cappadocia became a sunrise spectacle of floating balloons, it was a place of refuge.

Set in central Turkey, the region’s surreal rock formations were shaped by volcanic eruptions millions of years ago. Over time, wind and erosion sculpted the now famous fairy chimneys. But the real story of Cappadocia is not only above ground.

It is below it.

At Kaymaklı Underground City, eight levels stretch deep into the earth, four of them open to the public. Entire communities once lived here, complete with kitchens, storage rooms, ventilation shafts and chapels.

“Turkey is between Europe and Africa and Asia. Ancient armies marched through here all the time,” said Cappadocia tour guide Faruk Basibuyuk. “The people who used to live here had no chance against these armies. They had no jungle, nothing to hide in. So the people dug out caves generation by generation. It goes underground several levels for hiding.”

The largest underground city, Derinkuyu, could shelter up to 20,000 people.

Survival here was not accidental. It was architectural.

Sleeping Inside History

What was once protection has now become preservation.

The World of Travel team stayed at Cappadocia Cave Suites, part of the Kelebek Group, one of the pioneering hospitality brands that helped shape the region’s modern tourism economy while keeping its roots intact.

Ali Yavuz, owner of the Kelebek Group and founding partner of Turkish Heritage Travel, began with six rooms inside his family home in August 1993.

“I did my best to avoid letting big businesses and outsiders come here and only create cleaning jobs for people who were from here,” Yavuz said. “We kept everything local. For 30 years as I built my cave hotel business up, I showed my friends how to turn their land into a business and employ locals at every level. I am proud.”

He never imagined Göreme, his small hometown, would become a global destination.

That local first philosophy helped Cappadocia grow without losing itself.

A UNESCO Canvas of Faith

At the Göreme Open Air Museum, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, rock cut churches and monasteries carved directly into stone are decorated with frescoes dating from the 10th through 13th centuries.

These Byzantine paintings depicting the life of Jesus and the saints remain some of the best preserved examples of medieval Christian art in the world.

Cappadocia was not only a place people hid.

It was a place they worshipped.

Faith, like the rock itself, became embedded in the land.

The View From Above

Cappadocia’s most iconic image arrives at sunrise.

Hot air ballooning here dates back to the late 1960s, when French balloonists were inspired by the region’s unusual terrain and later organized the first festival. Today, hundreds of balloons rise into the sky each morning.

On this journey, the World of Travel team watched the sun spill across the valleys, revealing cave homes, churches, and hidden passageways from a perspective once unimaginable to the people who carved them.

From underground survival to sky high serenity, Cappadocia tells a story of transformation measured in altitude.

A Michelin Recognized Culinary Destination

Cappadocia’s evolution now includes global culinary recognition.

The Michelin Guide recently unveiled its “Top Travel Destinations for Food Lovers in 2026,” highlighting 16 regions around the world where gastronomy defines the experience. Among them is Cappadocia, an arid region of central Turkey that World of Travel explored firsthand.

Cappadocia’s food scene blends ancient Anatolian traditions with pottery-based cooking techniques that date back centuries. The iconic testi kebab is slow cooked inside a sealed clay pot and cracked open tableside. Delicate manti dumplings reflect layers of Central Asian influence and locally sourced ingredients.

At Seten Restaurant and Rooftop Bar, traditional dishes are served in a historic cave setting alongside tastings in a wine cellar carved into stone. Happena Restaurant pairs panoramic valley views with rich regional flavors.

Here, even the cooking is shaped by geology.

The Language of Scent

In Cappadocia, every craft carries memory.

During a perfume making workshop with Highlights in Cappadocia, we learned how fragrance traces back to Ottoman traditions where scent symbolized purification, hospitality, and social grace. Rosewater and kolonya were offered to guests as gestures of welcome. Fragrance was used in mosques and hammams to represent spiritual cleanliness.

Every merchant, every craft, every scent tells a story passed down through generations.

“This town calls you,” said Göreme resident Gonul Yavuz, partner in the Kelebek Group. “Once you come it calls you back. It is very special. The landscape, the culture, the hospitality, the people. It is special.”

Cappadocia does not just invite you to visit.

It invites you to return.

Bodrum: The Land of Eternal Blues

If Cappadocia is carved from earth, Bodrum rises from water.

Known in antiquity as Halicarnassus, Bodrum sits along Turkey’s Aegean coast and carries more than 3,500 years of recorded history. The poet Homer called it the Land of Eternal Blues.

Long before it became synonymous with summer yachts and whitewashed villas, this was one of the most important port cities of the ancient Caria region.

It is also the birthplace of Herodotus, known as the Father of History.

One of the Seven Wonders

Bodrum’s ancient identity is forever tied to the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus, one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.

Built around 353 BCE for Mausolus, ruler of Caria, by his wife Artemisia, the monumental tomb blended Greek, Egyptian, and Lycian architectural styles. The structure was so influential that the word mausoleum comes from his name.

Though earthquakes eventually destroyed it, remnants were later used in the construction of Bodrum Castle.

A Castle Built From History

Bodrum Castle, also known as St. Peter’s Castle, was constructed in 1402 by the Knights Hospitaller and today houses the Museum of Underwater Archaeology.

Inside are artifacts that trace maritime trade across the Mediterranean, including the 14th century BC Uluburun Shipwreck.

Haluk Arslan, our Turkey tour guide, explains that Bodrum’s story stretches across civilizations including the Lelegians, Carians, Persians, Dorians, Romans, Byzantines, and Ottomans. Alexander the Great captured the city in 334 BC. Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent incorporated it into the Ottoman Empire in 1522.

Civilizations did not just pass through Bodrum.

They built upon it.

Living Bodrum, Not Just Visiting It

The true way to experience Bodrum is locally.

Instead of staying in a resort, the World of Travel team stayed in a local home hosted by a Bodrum resident named Sema, embedding ourselves in the neighborhood and connecting with shop owners, fishermen, and families whose lives revolve around the sea. It completely transformed the experience.

Bodrum invites us to understand that transformation is not always about what is new.

Sometimes it is about what endures. The fishing traditions. The agriculture. The authentic architecture. The rhythm of life shaped by the Aegean.

The entertainment may last until morning. The coves may attract yachts from around the world. But beneath the glamour is something older and steadier.

A coastline that has witnessed empires rise and fall, yet remains timeless.

World of Travel explored two regions that expand the story of Turkey beyond any single headline. Cappadocia shows how communities carved survival into stone and turned it into sustainable tourism. Bodrum proves that even the site of a lost Wonder of the World can evolve into a living coastal culture.

From underground cities to underwater archaeology, from pottery sealed kebabs to ports that shaped ancient trade, Turkey continues to reveal itself in layers.

And in Cappadocia and Bodrum, we discovered this: Some places build monuments to be remembered.

Others endure, and become unforgettable.

EDITOR’S NOTE: Lindsey Granger and Kolyn Boyd are the co-creators of “World of Travel,” a series that

explores the past, present and future of extraordinary places around the globe. You can watch“ Bodrum & Cappadocia: Nature Resilience and Reinvention” and future episodes each Wednesday at 4 p.m. EDT on The Hill’s YouTube page.

Lindsey Granger is the co-host of “Rising.” Kolyn Boyd is a film director based in Washington, D.C.

Catch complimentary podcasts every Thursday at 4 p.m. on The Hill’s YouTube channel.

The Bodrum & Cappadocia episode of “World of Travel” was sponsored by Cappadocia Cave Suites, Kelebek Group and GetYourGuide
